boardgame: Paths of Peril: A Mysterious Island Card Game
description:
Paths of Peril: A Mysterious Island Card Game is a standalone game in which players will try to be the first to reach an objective point while facing dangers along the way. Play solo or up to 4 players.

A 7x7 grid of Explore cards becomes the "board". Players start with four Craft cards in hand and must cross the board using these items to keep you moving and out of danger... but the cards also count as life points, so don't run out!

This competitive game will have you strategizing your path and forcing other players to use up all their Craft cards. The first player to get to the other side of the board wins!

This is a card-based, COMPETITIVE adventure for survivors! Mysterious Island is not required to play Paths of Peril.

-description from designer

It is the dawn of interstellar travel for man. New technologies have been developed which allow interstellar star pilots to overcome challenges like stellar debris, warped space, and black holes. Mass production has finally brought the costs of these technological marvels down enough to be accessible to the private sector, and like any good space civilization you capitalize on the opportunity.

In Kepler Run, you play an independent space runner attempting to win the most riches for yourself by being the first to fulfill lucrative contracts the fastest. You'll need to put all of your runner know-how to good use, and with a little bit of planning and luck, you can be the first to complete your Kepler Run.

In more detail, Kepler Run is a card-drafting and route-planning game. Each round, new cards are revealed. In turn, players draft a card and decide whether to add it to their ship as an engine, add it to the board (the run) for points, or discard it for fuel modules. After each player has drafted two tiles, in turn players attempt to make it as far as they can, crossing from one side of the board to the other. Players collect points depending on how far they get and on which objective they complete along the way. After three rounds of drafting and running, the player with the most points wins.

—description from designer

A simple roll-and-move board game along a 100-space track, with bonuses and penalties based on eye safety. The winner is presumably the first to reach the final space, but the 'rules' consist of merely two sentences printed in the lower right of the board: "For 2-6 players. Throw the dice in turn and follow instructions."

The subtitle is "Can you SEE it safely through a day at Kangaroo Creek : throwing things, twirling things, sharp objects, eye injuries can cause blindness."

Bonuses include "You remember to use scissors without sharp points. Go ahead 6 spaces" and You throw a jellyfish in your friend's face. Go back 3 spaces."

Having no box or separate rules sheet, the game was probably produced for inclusion in showbags.
publishers: The Kangaroo Creek Gang company Pty. Ltd.

description:
Kill Team: Arena is an expansion for Warhammer 40,000: Kill Team that introduces rules for competitive play, using evenly balanced terrain set-ups and mission objectives to offer each player an even playing field. Also included are missions and rules for running competitive Kill Team tournaments, either for single players or teams of two allies (known as Battle Brothers).

The Book

A 64-page softback book, Kill Team: Arena explains everything you need to know about playing competitive games of Kill Team, from one-off games to various formats of tournament play:

Killzones: This sections describes rules for Ultra-close Confines combat and introduces four new Killzone Environments (Generatorium, Catacombs, Armorium Stockpile, and Garrison Vault), each with their own environmental rules.

Competitive Play: This section includes competitive rules, terrain maps and missions intended for use in games where balance is paramount, such as those played in tournaments.

Battle Brothers: Introduces a cooperative format for playing team-based competitive play games of Kill Team, where each Battle Brothers team consists of two players whose kill teams are pitted against their opponents' to see who can complete the most objectives. Includes 4 Battle Brothers missions and 28 Battle Brothers Tactics.

Ultima Missions: This section describes how to create custom competitive missions using the Killzones found in Kill Team: Arena by utilizing randomly generated killzones, deployment maps and Arena Objectives. This ensures no two competitive play games are exactly alike!

Kill Team Tournaments: Guidelines for running and participating in competitive play Kill Team tournaments, including how to register players and command rosters, determining player pairings in each round of a tournament, as well as how to score and rank players.

Using Competitive Play Missions in Campaigns: Guidance on how to use the missions from this set in your campaign games.

Extras

Along with the comprehensive book of rules, there is included a set of cards, tokens, scenery and game boards for use with Arena:

- 28 Battle Brothers Tactics cards
- 12 mission cards, including Battle Brothers missions and 8 Competitive Play missions
- 2 sets of 12 Arena Objective cards (one for each player). Each deck is also supplied with a storage sleeve to ensure they are not mixed up.
- 2 x 22x30” double-sided, folding card game boards
- 2 plastic frames of scenery, each containing the following: 5 doors, 4 barrels, 10 crates, 1 pipe, and 1 cylindrical container.

A copy of the Kill Team Core Manual is required to use this set.

The Interregnum is a science fiction strategy board game set in a time of cold conflict between competing stellar kingdoms in a fallen Galactic Empire. The 2-to-6 players win by dominating the other kingdoms and earning the seat of Emperor, either by building a military hegemony or a powerful merchant empire. Both paths to success require careful planning, resource management and cunning strategy.

You will take the reins of one of the six Star Kingdoms that are part of the fallen Empire, ruled by the leaders that have the best claim to the throne after the late Emperor’s demise: The Duchess, The Princess, The Marquis, The Baron, The Caliph, and The Khan.

Your mission is to guide your people to the stars, secure vital resources to support your growth, make important decisions that will forge your Kingdom’s fate, and take back what’s rightfully yours – the title of Basileus, Emperor of Mankind.

The Interregnum is played on a modular map divided into hexagonal Sectors and Solar Systems. These are each further divided into 3 smaller tiles that allow for a better random distribution of Planets and Resources on the map and also keeping the scale Galactic yet detailed enough to be enjoyable.

The Interregnum gameplay is structured into Rounds that each represent a galactic standard year. Within one Round, all players have a Turn – meaning that a Round is further divided into 2 to 6 Turns.

Within a Turn, a player has 1 or 2 Actions (depending on their position on the ISC’s Turn/Dividend track). Actions are the following: Moving Fleets or Building. If a player has two actions he/she may move twice or move and build in the same turn.

During their Turn, a player may also do any of the following: trade once with the Galactic Market, repay their own debts, acquire loans, sell loans, buy a Technology card, use any number of cards, change their focus (but only when you are the Board Director). These are not considered Actions and can be done at any time during your turn after receiving your resource input.

While there is no actual combat in The Interregnum, players are required to use their Fleets in order to occupy Sectors and build structures inside them.  Generally speaking, Fleets move on the between the Sectors – called Hyperlanes. They can also be “Parked” within a Sector (Deep space or Solar Orbit), but cannot move from Sector to Sector while Parked.

Building Fleets, Miner/Traders or Space Stations use up Resources (gained from Deep Space resource tiles or from inhabited planets) and Research points (gained from Citizen or Immigrant population)

The game ends when one of the players gathers 5 Reform Score (Mercantile or Hegemonic) through any means.

Once the end is triggered, your victory points are determined by your populations and assets. These are scored differently depending on which path you take when reforming your Star Kingdom into an Empire.

Hegemonic Empire path rewards military expansion while keeping the power in the hands of the few and alike. Citizen and Colonist Populations along with finished Space Stations add to your score, while Immigrants and Offworlders reduce your final score.
Merchant Empire path rewards population diversity and powerful Trader presence. Offworlders and Immigrants along with Trade Stations add to your score, and indeed Citizens and Colonists reduce your score.

-description from designer

Game covers battle fought at the 6 September 1939 at Tomaszów Mazowiecki in the central Poland between Polish 13 Infantry Division and German 1st Armored Division. Polish positions near this town were the last major obstacle for the German armored units on their way to Warsaw. Thanks to the quick attack and diversion, Germans managed to break through the Polish defense in the one day, destroying most of the Polish units.

Game contains two scenarios: historical one and hypthetical one that includes some Polish units that didn't fought in the battle but could be used.

Game was published in the 34th issue of the "Taktyka i Strategia" magazine. It uses "B-35" battalion/company scale system.
family: Series: Bitwy II wojny światowej 1943-45 (Dragon / Taktyka i Strategia)

description:
"Wizna 1939" covers the battle of Wizna fought during the Second World War Polish campaign between Polish garrison of the Wizna and German 10th Armored Division at 8-9 september 1939. Despite the desperate odds, Poles managaed to stop Germans for almost three days, thanks to the muddy terrain and bunkers.

Battle is often called "Polish Thermopylae". A Sabaton wrote a song about this battle titled "40:1".

Wizna 1939 contains three scenarios: two historical ones and one hypothetical that allows Polish player to use more units. Game uses "B-35" battalion/company scale system.

Wizna 1939 was published in the 42 issue of "Taktyka i Strategia", a Polish wargaming magazine.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Series: Bitwy II wojny światowej 1943-45 (Dragon / Taktyka i Strategia)

description:
The game takes place in Southern seas of a fairyland. You make a decision to try your luck, fit out a schooner and set your sail towards the South. After long and painful days of sailing towers of a large port town appear in the horizon...

In the game, each participant assumes the role of the owner of the ship, on which he sails around the bay, depicted on the colorful playing field.

Players have quite a lot of different opportunities: buy / sell goods in cities, improve their ships, buy guns, seize cities, invest their money in bills of exchange, build forts, and of course, fight each other.

The rules of the game are very simple (but not primitive) and are intended, rather, for children. In addition, the game does not have a goal as such: we simply sail across the bay, enriching ourselves by trading and looting each other, trying to surpass the wealth of our opponents.

Players usually set their own rules for the game, ether to accumulate a set amount of wealth or fight other players until they don't have money to buy new supplies.

However, this is one of the few Russian-made board games in the 1990s, which will be really interesting for the participants to play: calculate where to swim, how to avoid or impose a battle, and how to protect your savings with promissory notes.

The spirit of sea adventure calls you.

Shuffle the card to the other Badlands cards when setting up the game.
When you see this card, IMMEDIATELY begin rolling your dice to get 3 stars. The last player to show 3 stars on their dice injures 1 scavenger.

Runerunners is a loot-hunting skirmish board game set in a high fantasy world, with a twist. The game takes place in a 1920's "gangster" era where Prohibition is in full swing, making the use or trade of any item with magical properties against the law.

Players form small teams of heroic Runerunners to scour the streets of Sanctuary City, taking jobs to hunt down illegal artifacts, before rival teams can get them first. Teams are formed at random and each character will move about the board, exploring the town and looking for clues, but sometimes the easiest way to score an item is to steal from your enemies, or defeat them in battle and run off with all of their loot.

The game features large double-sided board tiles that can be arranged any way you wish, creating a different map every time you play. Your team of Heroes has taken a contract to search for contraband magical items. There will be a variety of different jobs or game modes, allowing you to have a regular battle game, or take an hold specific areas of the board, or search for clues to find special items. The rulebook will feature many different scenarios, and include instructions for how to create your own custom games as well.

Each board tile features a 9x9 grid for movement. Both players alternate activating one hero at a time, and each hero has a set number of actions where they can move, search for clues, and steal from or attack enemy heroes. Each Hero may only be activated once per game turn, and the game will typically last for 6 full turns. Whoever can score the most kills or gather the most loot before the game ends will win.

Runerunners is designed to be simple to learn, but also tactical and strategic, where each hero's unique skills can make or break a mission.

-description from designer

In Softwar, players are electronics intelligence operators, or in common parlance cyberspies. Their job is to assemble, deploy and control teams of specialist digital programs into the world’s electronic networks. 
Their aim is to access a set of key assets protected by their opponent, while simultaneously defending their own assets from enemy attack.

Players choose and control a set of programs, each of which is represented by a dice. The value of any program is called its state.
A program’s state is reduced as it takes damage; if it ever lowers below 1, that program is immediately eliminated from the game.

After setting up the board and assembling their teams, players take turns to run all of their programs. Programs come in two forms, Agents and Crypts. 
Agents are active, movable pieces. Crypts on the other-hand are immobile; sat atop assets like protective turrets. Crypts are arguably more limited than agents, though unlike their mobile cousins, they also serve as an advanced asset deployment platform.

Every agent may take a number of standard actions including moving, supporting (recharging their teammates) and attacking. In addition to these standard actions, each agent has a single specialised action, chosen by its owner before the start of the game. Players use these specialised actions to create advantages and opportunities to successfully win the game.

A player wins the game by either eliminating three out of four of their opponents Crypts (granting them access to the assets on which they sit) or by completely destroying their opponents team of agents.

Components include:

     35 dice per player in a variety of types (D4, D6, D8, D10, D12 and D20)
     A batch crafted set of a board and tokens. 
     A range of 15 special action cards per player, each of which may be applied to any dice type per player.


—description from the pulbisher

The game - draw cards, make haikus with cards in hand, preform them for the group, and have fun!

To start the game each player draws three 5 syllable cards and two 7 syllable cards. One player will read to the group a "Performance Card". The Performance Card will give all players a note on how they should perform their Haiku for the rest of the group.

Based on this Performance Card, players will use their cards to create a 5,7,5 Haiku. Players may use either side of the 5 and 7 cards to create their poem.

The player who initially read the Performance card will select their favorite based on both the haiku itself and/or the performance.

Each deck comes with 550 Cards! The deck is comprised of 100 performance prompt cards and 450 double sided haiku cards.

You have the treasure in your hands.  Now you need to escape!  This is a solo print-and-play game where you play as an adventurer trying to escape a dungeon.  But both Time and a wide array of Creatures are not on your side!

Equipped with a set of 7 dice, a pencil, and an eraser you will attempt your escape.  On each turn, depending on the roll of dice, you will move, combat creatures, and loot the dungeon for equipment and spells that will aid you in your escape.  However, with each turn you will also roll to track the passage of time.  Escape, and you win!  But first you must survive the treasure-guarding creatures, and make it to the exit to the dungeon before the clock fills up.

-description from designer

Star Waves is a tabletop card game that sends you on an awesome journey through space,
which requires basic resource management, strategy and dice luck. Beat your opponent
in completing a wild series of Quests and establish yourself as the most famous bounty hunter
in the solar system.



The game is portable, quick to set up and takes less than 5 minutes to learn.
Whip it out whenever you feel like exploring locations across the universe and experiencing
all sorts of amazing and mischievous events along the way.




• Choose a Quest Card. Each player gains their Starting Resources.




• Discover a new Location every turn. Then, roll a dice to find great treasure—or total disaster.
You never know what you encounter next and how it will play out. 




• At the beginning of your turn, draw an Action Card.
Play them to sabotage your opponents, or accelerate your own progress.




• Win the game by completing all Quests, such as recovering Artifacts or earning Credits.


—description from the designer

Each starter pack includes:
– 3 exclusive NHL 2.5-inch figures with their player stat cards
– 6 dice
– 3 pucks
– Playing mat
– Rule book for the game (also available to download).

A game can be played with as few as 3 action figures (1 goalie, 1 forward and 1 defenseman) for each player. However, it is highly recommended to play with a complete team of 6 action figures (1 goalie, 3 forwards, 2 defensemen). A team cannot total more than 25 points. Please refer to the skills cards of each action figure to determine the points each is valued at and consider this guideline in making up your team.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: KeyForge: Age of Ascension – Archon Deck
description: KeyForge: Age of Ascension – Archon Deck consists of a single unique deck created from cards in the Age of Ascension set, the second set of cards in the KeyForge line. Each player must have their own deck in order to play the game.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
It's been 84 years since the great Kaiju Wars. Humanity has made many advancements and discoveries since that dark time. However no revelation was as ground breaking as the discovery of the islands. Beautiful Oblivion, one of three islands, was formed by the synthesis of earth's geography with the fallen enemy of our past and with it a treasure trove of new resources. Harvesting these resources changed our future and quite possibly sealed our fate. Because of our ignorance a new gate is now forming and it is only a matter of time before a breach occurs unleashing Kaijus hell bent on destroying us all.

In Beautiful Oblivion, a strategy board game for 2-5 players, you are the master engineer tasked with creating the next great mech (Krieger) to save us all. Your team of engineers are sent out to harvest the resources needed in order to construct such modern marvels. These new resources come with a cost though, as over harvesting only accelerates the end game.

During the course of play, you'll gain points by using those resources to build or upgrade your Krieger. Choose to follow a blueprint for additional points or shoot from the hip on your design. All the while, navigating world events that can help you or stop you dead in your tracks. The points you earn translate to the strength of your Krieger. Building bigger is stronger, but much slower to do. A light upgraded Krieger might do the trick but will it be strong enough. Either way you'll need to hurry, once the gate is inevitably opened, ending the game, only completed Kriegers will fight for glory against a variety of Kaijus for the possibility to gain bonus points. The player with the most points wins the game.

-description from designer

A game played on a board bearing illustrations of 48 black and red playing cards (in a 6 × 8 grid), with Aces, then Kings at the top, down to 9s at the foot. There are numbered circles along each edge of the board, which is green / cream in colour.

It was published in England in the 1920s by Unicorn Products Ltd.

There is a copy in London's V&A (Victoria & Albert) Museum, as part of their Museum of Childhood collection.

Rules and other playing accessories are not known.

The game 花式自殺 (Fancy Suicide) bears the subtitle "十萬個激嬲女友的理由", which could be translated as "1001 Ways to Provoke Your Girlfriend". Not sure what the game is about, but perhaps we'll all learn in time.

boardgame: Ashes Reborn: The Protector of Argaia
description:
Fiona Mercywind has stood by long enough, and The Phoenixborn of Birdsnest can no long stay out of the battle raging across Argaia.

Like other Phoenixborn, she has felt the drive to challenge others for supremacy, but the War of Ashes continues to destroy and the druidic orders of Birdsnest have called upon Fiona to right the wrongs of her fellow Phoenixborn. Tap into the minds of your enemies thanks to the Ashes: The Protector of Argaia' expansion to confuse and dispatch them, or summon powerful conjurations in the form of wizened animal companions. Fiona's unique blend of Sympathy and Charm magic will lead her to victory as the Protector of Argaia!
family: Game: Ashes – Rise of the Phoenixborn

description:
James Endersight is no stranger to suffering, and his resolve has been steeled by the losses he's endured. The Phoenixborn of Marrowden, available in the Ashes: The Grave King expansion, leads an army of undead allies into battle in order overwhelm his enemies and claim dominion over all of Argaia. As his foes are twisted by Endersight's dark magic, they eventually join the ranks of his shambling hordes as they march towards the next battle. Call upon powerful Ceremonial and Divine magic to lead your undead forces in the name of the Grave King!

—description from the publisher
family: Game: Ashes – Rise of the Phoenixborn

description:
In this party game 2 teams play against each other. The goal of the game is to get rid of all his EMOTIS. 
For this every team is given a writing board, a felt-tip pen and a screen. Without looking, every team take 10 EMOTIS chips from a bag and place them behind their screen.
The team that starts chooses a player who spins the hand of a rotating dial and carries out the instructions. The dial is split into 10 fields:

     6 themes with a maximum number of EMOTIS(Leisure, People, Places, Actions, Brands and Friends)
     4 special fields (Open Theme, Gifting, Removing, Swapping)


If the hand points to a theme, the choosen player turns over the timer, puts together a row of EMOTIS behind the screen (to represent the name/title etc. of something within the theme), writes the answer down on the writing board (without any of the other players seeing it) and removes the screen.
Now all players of his team have to guess what it is in the given time. In addition, the choosen player can give his team a given hint (find on the inside of the screen). If the team guesses correctly, the EMOTIS used are removed from the game. If not, the team take (without looking) as many EMOTIS from the bag as they have used and the opposing team may try to give a correct answer (if correct, the opposing team remove as many EMOTIS from their pile as the other team used). Then the turn goes to the other team.
If the hand points to a special field, the choosen player take, deposit or exchange of EMOTIS respectively give one of your EMOTIS to your opponent and choose a theme of your own.

—user summary

With over 54 comic book cards featuring all original artwork, 38 powers to gain & allowing up to 8 players, there is plenty of fun to be had in Palm City. The game manual also features a Palm City History section you can fill out that keeps track of the winners and their game stats. So family and friends can forever remember your victory... Hero or Villain.


        HOW TO PLAY
    


Players will roam the Palm City streets on this 8x8 grid gameboard, using powers they gain to complete city events. At the start of the game all players will be competing against one another as heroes. Frantically moving around the board trying to complete city events before other players beat them to it. Each of the 54 city events are original and will make this city feel alive. City events could be anything from saving a family in a burning home, stopping a meteor from crashing into Palm City, or helping the mayor's cat out of a tree. Each city event requires certain powers to complete though, so players will also be competing to obtain genetic mutations around the board that grant powers. Palm City is full of stuff happening where your help is always needed but you know what they say. Live long enough... and you'll find yourself the villain. At any point in the game, a player may attack another player. The moment a hero player causes damage to another hero player, they become a villain. There is no going back from this. At this point in the game, your only objective as a villain, is to eliminate all other players using the powers you've gained. Each power in the game has a unique ability and with 38 of them, make sure you build your character carefully. All of this happens within a player's turn and a turn consists of two phases.
 Phase 1: Move
Phase 2: Complete Action

Actions that can be completed include: Completing a city event, gaining a genetic mutation, trading & discarding genetic mutations, applying powers, or attacking another player.

Win the game as a hero by completing city events found on comic book cards and getting your city respect to 100 before any other player.

Win the game as a villain by using your powers to bring the health of other players to zero, destroying them all, and taking over Palm City.

This is a large game featuring a lot of content. At first it might feel overwhelming, but the manual should easily guide you through the process of learning how to setup & play. Once you learn, you'll never want to leave Palm City.


        GAME STORY
    


You and your neighbors notice something break apart in the sky outside the city. All of you gather and travel to the wreckage. You discover a glowing purple material. Something never before seen. It calls to you. It calls to each of you. One by one you all touch it. Everyone feels different. Everyone feels powerful. You realize each time you touch it, your genetics are mutating.
You're becoming stronger. You all decide it's best to keep this wreckage site a secret. The only question though, is what you'll do with your new powers. Will you become palm city's greatest hero... or its most feared villain?

Welcome to Palm City.

Drakberget is a card driven duel between two players, age 10 and up, where the goal is to capture the opponent's heir while protecting their own.

Players choose their movement secretly and perform them simultaneously.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Anno Urbis: The Fight for Rome
description:
Ancient Rome, IV century A.D.: it is a stormy period in Roman Empire, the quiet of past centuries is only a memory and the struggle for the title of Emperor is increasingly harsh.
In the Capital of the Empire, the most important "Gens" do not miss a chance to try to increase their power, to be able to influence the new Augustus as much as possible. Despite political instability, each faction wants to enlarge its possessions and is not afraid to face also armed clashes, with bloody battles later forgotten in the windings of the official historiography... Meanwhile, the Arena and the gladiators are important faces in the fight for the power: the people worship their idols!

Anno Urbis - The Fight for Rome is a strategy and combat board game, set in a period where the power of emperors is often challenged by ambitious province governors or by smart military commanders. In Rome, the Senate is torn apart by the struggle among the various factions, while the people can be influenced by the charisma of the heroes fighting in the Arena. You will have to balance the development of each settlement, the conquest of new territories (even through military battles) and the growth of the skills of your Hero, so to control the most of the Eternal City’s seven hills: only in this way it will be possible to dominate Rome and, perhaps in the future, even the entire Empire...

In Anno Urbis - The Fight for Rome, each player will command a powerful Roman dynasty and will have to struggle to gain the domination over the Capital of the Empire: build barracks and farms and expand your influence on the “domus” on the seven hills of Rome, but do not forget to use your resources to erect a temple in honor of one of the deities! In return, you will have incredible relics for your hero, as well as unique powers for gaining an advantage and defeat your rivals. But do not underestimate the Arena, if you manage to accumulate more glory, the whole city could be yours, but if you were defeated... well, in Rome the losers have a short life!

Anno Urbis - The Fight for Rome offers exceptional components, many miniatures and the 3D models of the seven hills of Rome, true focus of the game and whose conquest will determine the winner. Next to them there is also the Gladiator Arena, in which the players’ fighters will compete to the death to increase glory and influence of their faction. The union among resources management, cards and combat make this game unique, but also the historical accuracy is important: indeed, we tried to rely on really existed events, characters and places, to allow players to immerse themselves in a sometimes neglected, but also fascinating and glorious, historical period.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: The Murder House: One. Two. Flee.
description:
The Murder House is a survival game where you are trapped inside an evil house that constantly spawns vile creatures and horrible monsters at you. Your mission as a captive inside this house of horrors is to be the first and only survivor.

In The Murder House, you spend your turn searching the different rooms, collecting Item cards. These functions as a sort of currency in the game. Currency which can be spent on different Gear. Gears can be anything from a medpack, a helmet, goggles, that grant you new abilities. If these are combined correctly, you will have a Set. The Sets offer a huge bonus to these abilities.

The Murder House offers you free movement, based on a limited set of movement points. These points can be permanently or passively be either increased or decreased based on effects given through either Action cards or Gear and Sets.

As you plan your escape, you will be hunted by villains. Each round, a Villain card is drawn - revealing the location of the Villains. If you want to make it out alive, you'll need to make use of the many hiding places inside the house: Shower curtains, fireplaces, underneath tables and inside cryo tanks - when the Villains show up, you do NOT want to be seen.

—description from the designer

For almost 600 years, from the fabled shores of the Bosphorus, the Ottoman Sultans ruled over a vast land in Europe, stretching from the Mediterranean to the Danube.  Sometimes enlightened, sometimes cruel, the rule of the Chalifs of Islam had steadily receded before the fury of national ideals and the rising might of European imperialism promoted by bayonet and capital. And yet it still survived, still stubbornly clinging to Balkan territories rich in history, industry and population. The “Great Patient” seemed to spite Greece, Bulgaria, Serbia and Montenegro; the states created by bloody struggle by those who once were termed the gavur rayahs of the Sultan. They saw with worry the growing appetite of aggressive great powers, all making claims to what they saw as their just inheritance. And then by coup and counter-coup, the fall of a Red Sultan, and the rise of a Committee, the rapaciousness of a young Great Power, and the entente of two old foes, the Balkan states saw danger and opportunity and struck. In the span of just a month of the greatest battles seen in Europe since 1871, the dream of Osman was shattered.

This scenario supplement for Bloody Big Battles! permits you to re-fight those great battles that saw the end of the Ottoman Empire in Europe and the formation of the basic modern borders in the Balkans. Nine scenarios cover some of the most important battles of the First Balkan War, covering the campaigns of Greece, Serbia and Bulgaria against the Ottoman Empire which would drive the Empire to the very gates of Istanbul/Constantinopole/Tsarigrad.  Five scenarios then cover the Second Balkan War, the conflict between the successful allies which pitted Serbia, Montenegro and Greece against Bulgaria over the legacy of the Ottoman Empire.

Each scenario provides a distinct operational and tactical puzzle for war-gamers to resolve. From straightforward assaults against fortified positions like the First Battle of Chataldja, to complex double envelopments like the Battle of Lule Burgas, from the extensive broad fronts of the Battle of Bregalnitza, to the knife fight in close quarters of the Battle of Bizani. There are small scenarios easily played in one or two hours, like the Battle of Dioran (the actual First battle of Dioran), and more substantive scenarios like the Battle of Kumanovo, still playable in an evening.  There are battles fought on rolling hills and plains, like the Battle of Kirkkilise, and battles fought in some of the worst ground people have ever thought of fighting each other in an organized manner, like the Battle of Kalimanchi.

You will see how these wars where similar and how they differed from The Great War. You will understand the crucial role of terrain as a military variable and grapple with key decisions of force to task distributions in an unforgiving strategic operational and tactical environment. You will see the last glory days of the bayonet charge, and the opening bloody howl of modern artillery.  There is challenge to satisfy all types of war-gamers.

An included overview of the historical events and biographies of many of the commanders whose roles you reprise on the table-top will provide you with the background to give you an inkling of the vast stakes and human drama associated with these battles. An annotated bibliography provides you both with the sources used to build the scenarios and further reading to enrich your understanding and experience. A figure guide helps you find suitable ways to represent the armies in almost all scales of miniature war-gaming.

A struggle for a vast future awaits! Take command and see how history was forged!

—description from the publisher

War Along the Great Lakes is Volume III in Worthington Games War of 1812 series.

The game uses blocks and point to point movement to recreate the fighting along the Great Lakes during the War of 1812 between the United States and Great Britain.  A British loss in the Great Lakes could have easily meant Canada would be part of the United States today.

During each turn each player receives action points plus random action points to activate his units for movement and combat. During combat each unit rolls dice and scores hits on opponent units.  Careful planning is required not to get trapped.

Victory is determined by occupation of key terrain features and elimination of enemy units.

Due to the randomness of the action points no two games play the same.  For this reason the game plays great solitaire (no solo engine - but by playing both sides).

—description from the publisher
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Series: War Along... War of 1812 (Worthington Publishing)

description:
In Judgement, players assume the role of one of the five 'Everlasting', demigods who live on the Shadow Plane known as Between. Each of the five were once champions of their race before transcending from mortals to demigods while on a quest to destroy the gods of old.

Their quest was bestowed upon them at The Gathering, a time when the major races of Athien came together to select five among them, so they could travel to the plane of shadow and stop the meddling of the old gods. The orcs and dwarfs nominated their most respected spiritual leaders, Krognar and Bruell to guide the five. The Elves and Minotaurs nominated their most powerful mage, Ista, and warrior Grul, to challenge the gods. By consensus the humans provided their greatest knight, Tomas, to provide leadership for the company.

As one of the Everlasting, you summon heroes from the mortal world of Athien to the shadow plane (Between), so they can harvest souls at your behest. Souls that give you power and sustain your immortality. This is the game of Judgement.

Judgement brings all the elements of the Multiplayer Online Battle Arena (MOBA) video game genre to the table top. Powerful, diverse and fun heroes, neutral monsters, magical items and hero levels. These ingredients provide a truly amazing gaming experience like no other miniature game on the market. The game design team worked tirelessly for almost 2 years creating, testing and perfecting the core mechanics which ensure diverse, dynamic hero activations and a fast, flexible combat system. The icing on the cake are superbly detailed 54mm scale resin cast miniatures.

—description from the publisher

F.O.E. - Fortitude of Empires is a sci-fi dice battle board game, set in a solar system with multiple planets and 4 warring factions: The Narr, Eldin, Solitude and Sorn empires. The object of the game is to take over as many planets in the system while crushing the other empires and holding on to the planets you already occupy. Each turn players have the option to move one of their fleet of upto five ships, collect credits from occupied planets, terraform planets or install planet defences, upgrade their pilots, attack other empires (planets and ships) using battle dice and 6 dials ( Weapons / Shield / Armour / Agility / Special / Promotion ) on their empire’s combat boards which surround the main playing area. The battles can easily turn on the throw of a dice or the turning of a card.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Tempus Quest: Episode 0 – Some Re-Assembly Required
description:
After evading local law enforcement, the crew of the Tempus find their beloved ship in a starport impound lot, in considerably more pieces than they last saw her. The crew must find a way to escape the planet, but in order to leave, they must first build six specific ship parts and install them in the ship without alerting the guards.

Games in the Tempus series work similarly to roll and write games where players roll dice and use the results to fill in a sheet. In the Tempus System, instead of using dice, players will use the date and time to randomize setup and available actions.

—description from the designer

Mechanical toy game, consisting of a plastic dome-shaped mound with a spring-loaded rubber bunny pushed into an opening at the top. Along the curved sides, plastic carrots are mounted into a series of holes. A mechanism inside randomly assigns one carrot as trigger, as the bunny is pushed down into place.

Players take turns spinning the spinner, and depending on the result, pulls one or more carrots out. Points are awarded for carrots as well as managing to catch the bunny as it is triggered (ending the game) and it jumps out.

—user summary

CatFish is an Improvisational-Storytelling Party Game which takes place over the course of a ridiculous dinner date. In this Cat-Eat-Cat world, if you're not the predator, you're the prey.

A group of nefarious cons and cheats, known simply as The Catfish, have found their new target - a cute, little, naive internet dater - their perfect Patsy.

These cheats have all put on quite the charade in order to collar the Patsy's attention while chatting them up on their favorite dating site www.CatFishDating.com - Now they are all in the unenviable position of having to back up their outlandish claims in person. They must fight tooth-and-nail and summon every bit of charm, wit, and creativity they have in order to win the affection of The Patsy.

In CatFish! The Patsy will play as the moderator, judge and acting coach of the game, while the Catfish will play as improvisational actors.

In this dinner-date of fictitious personalities and backstories, the Catfish that attains the most Appeal Points over three-courses of unadulterated lies and manipulation, will be chosen as the winner
of the game!

They'll do so by masterfully Drafting the Claim Cards and Hobby Cards that they think they can weave into the most captivating backstory - and then whimsically acting out the character that they just created in order to impress the Patsy and match what they're Looking For in a mate.

It won't be easy, because both the Patsy and the other Catfish will relentlessly try to poke holes in their stories, and question their identities. Those Catfish that are best able to think on their feet 
and Improvise the details of their backstory will go far.

Who will win the Patsy's heart, and who will be outed as a filthy stinkin' Catfish!?

In Mutation Wars you and your opponents are competing to be the first to develop a creature that can rule the ocean depths!

All players begin with a tiny creature — one body (tile) and must build it up as the game is played. To do this, each turn all players receive new cards from three different decks. The cards are used in different phases of the turn. A turn plays as follows:


     Cards are dealt to all players — one each from the Body Part, Mutate and Battle card decks
     Build Phase — players can use their Body Part cards to add a body part to their creature or purchase a new body (tile) with Body Part cards
     Mutate Phase — players can use their Mutate cards to change their creature, change their opponent's creatures or affect all creatures for the current turn
     Battle Phase — players can attack other creatures in an attempt to steal their Mutate and Battle cards; players can use their Battle cards to help them increase their stats and thus their possibility of winning
     Cleanup Phase — players can exchange three Mutate cards or three Battle cards for one Body Part card


The game is won when a player succeeds in having a winning creature survive until the end of a turn. A winning creature requires four bodies (tiles) and six body parts (cards attached to the tiles) and must also have a color-matching body part on each of the external bodies. Should multiple players create a winning creature in the same turn and have them survive to the end of the turn, whoever built it first is the winner.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Legend of the Five Rings: The Card Game – For the Empire
description:
Take to the battlefield with For the Empire, the first Dynasty Pack in the Inheritance Cycle for Legend of the Five Rings: The Card Game!

For the Empire includes one copy of three new cards, and three copies of nineteen new cards for Legend of the Five Rings: The Card Game. This Dynasty Pack focuses on the brave Bushi who fight for their clans on battlefields strewn with blood and dirt. Furthermore, look for the introduction of the new disguised mechanic, which sees hidden iconic samurai take on the identities of unassauming non-unique characters, only to reveal their true skill and ability at the most opportune moment. All of this and for awaits you in For the Empire!

-description from the publisher's website
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)
Setting: Legend of the Five Rings

description:
New factions and battlefields join the world of Root!

The Underworld Expansion includes:

    The Great Underground Duchy: an imperial faction that mixes the flexibility of the Marquise with the escalating Eyrie Dynasties.
    The Corvid Conspiracy: a secretive faction that hatches plots directly into the hands of their opponents.
    Two new maps: dig tunnels on the mountain map or fight to control the ferry on the lake map.


—description from the publisher

boardgame: Root: The Exiles and Partisans Deck
description:
Mix things up with an alternative deck of cards. This deck replaces the old deck and contains a fresh roster of special powers for players to craft, many based on the factions themselves. Frustrated by the Woodland Alliance's guerrilla tactics? Develop the ability to fight in the shadows yourself! But, be careful, many new powers come with new vulnerabilities.

—description from the publisher

description:
Snotnose High is a co-op role-playing board game where 2-4 players take on the roles of typical high schoolers - the Jock, the Nerd, the Gamer, the Goth, the Black Guy, and the Dyke. 
Their mission? Rescue the self-proclaimed-hottest-girl-at-school Britney Blonde who's recently been kidnapped by the evil Professor Blender.
Their mood? Pissed. 
Their genitalia? Hardened (with the notable exception of the Dyke.)
Within his blender, Professor Blender has concocted a menagerie of unique monsters, different each time the game is played, to stop our heroes. Will he prevail?

Players will travel across Snotville, an island situated between  the mystical lands of Atlantis, Middle Earth, and the People's Republic of Kuwait. Landmarks at Snotville consists of Snotnose High itself, the rival highschool Harvord High, the Suburbs where everyone lives, the Arcade where we can all play games within the game, and the History Hut, a bar frequented exclusively by prominent figures of history, like Albert Einstein, Charles Darwin, and John Cena.

Along the way, they will encounter, count, and account for countless Count Dracula-like creatures, all with a different head, body, and pair of legs, as blended in Professor Blender's blender.
But they'll have to hurry - the evil Professor intends to blend Britney herself!

—description from the designer

boardgame: Robinson Crusoe: Adventures on the Cursed Island – Volcano Tile Promo
description:
This promo originally came in the Polish version of Teotihuacan, and later in the Tantrum House Season 8 Kickstarter.

It is used in Scenario 4 (Volcano Island). As a part of the setup phase place this mini expansion on the volcano tile. During the first 3 rounds you can use explore action on this tile - only 1 action per round allowed. If successful, choose one of the three awards: resolve two Mystery Cards (draw only treasure chests), increase weapon level by 2, draw 2 Starting Items. Then put a black marker on this tile, it's no longer available. At the beginning of round 4 remove this tile from the game.
family: Game: Robinson Crusoe – Adventures on the Cursed Island

description: To flip with two jettons instead of dice-throwing and walk to the castle

Vexed is a turn-based dice game for 2 or more players.  The object of the game is to become the first player to score 200 points by rolling dice that match those already in the central pool.  After each successful roll the player must decide whether to collect their score so far or risk losing everything in the hope of scoring more.

As long as a player keeps matching with dice in the central pool they can keep rolling and accumulating points.  However, if they push their luck too far and roll a die that doesn't match with anything they score zero and all of their previously matched dice are returned to the central pool, leaving a bumper scoring opportunity for the next player.

-description from publisher

boardgame: The Dutch East Indies: Adventures on the High Seas
description:
Adventures of the High Seas, the first expansion for The Dutch East Indies, features six modules that can be integrated into the base game one at a time, or all at once. The expansion is compatible with both the standard and deluxe edition of the base game. These are the new modules, as depicted on the top of the box, going clockwise:

1 – Forts. In the base game, you have to watch out for pirates and other players while sailing, and nothing else. When you deliver your spices back on your home island, they are safe. Now, not anymore… You start with a fort on your home island with which you can defend against other players trying to steal your spices. Pirates get 2 forts on the High Seas to aid them, but if you defeat them, you could own those forts!

2 – Settlements. Because this expansion comes with a lot more options to spend your gold on, it’s only fair that you also get the opportunity to earn more. You can now build settlements on wild islands with no cards left on them. The settlement will produce crates for you, and act as a harbor to protect you from attacks too!

3 – Treasures. Similarly to settlements, when you find a treasure, you find gold! Which aids you wih modules 1 and 6.

4 – Events. No longer can you only draw trade propositions at wild islands. When you least expect it, lightning strikes! Or you summon the Kraken. Or who knows, something helpful may happen. It also means you can no longer safely look at the last card at an island. It may be an event, which means then the deck is empty and you can be attacked!

5- Reefs. Another danger on the High Seas for you, your opponents and the pirates! Are you going to be safe and sail around them, or take the risk and hope your ship passes safely through it? Getting stuck is no fun, except for when it happens to the pirates!

6- Crew. When an island runs out of trade cards in the base game, they are of no interest. Not anymore! You can now build settlements, but the locals on the island are also looking for something to do! Pay a local for their services and upgrade your ship in a whole new way! Just be careful in battles not to get your crew killed!

-description from publisher

description:
Legends is a small scale high action skirmish game of super heroic improbable action.

It is a multi player game requiring a set of D6 and 1 to 3 suitably heroic miniatures. It plays out on a 2x2' table with as much urban terrain as players can muster. A game runs to roughly 30 minutes per player.

It puts players into the shoes of any lycra clad hero they might imagine, or other super powered individual, in a battle of extreme proportions. Models can run up buildings to launch themselves into the sky, slam through walls and pick up sky scrapers to hit their opponents with. Inspired by both the world of Marvel superheros and the extreme action of films such as Kung Fu Hustle Legends creates a world in which almost any imaginable action is possible with only a handful of mechanics and a couple of pages of rules. 

—description from the publisher

In Wizards of War you play an evil wizard and your goal is to defeat the other wizard by casting spells and summon monsters.

Each turn you roll the action die to see what you can do that turn. If you roll a staff you get a spell, if you roll an orc you get a monster (summoned directly and placed on the board) and if you roll the feet you can move your wizard / monsters and attack (or cast a spell if it's the wizard).

The battle takes place on a board (containing 11 x 20 squares) with terrain affecting movement and combat.

Fighting goes as follows: The attacker does damage based on the amount of swords that are rolled. The defender blocks damage based on the amount of shields that are rolled. The difference is then subtracted from the defender’s life total. The wizards has 4 lives, the monsters all have one. If the defender survives the attack he gets one counterattack.

—description from the designer

Three policemen try to catch 3 vagabonds. Figures are caught, if they are encircled, so that they are not able to move. Caught figures are eliminated from the game. Movement is on the black spaces of the game board, straight in any direction as much spaces as possible. First the 3 policemen are moved, then the 3 vagabonds.

The player with the last figure on the game plan wins the game.

—user summary

Prepare to embark on an epic journey as you ascend the slopes of the Himalayan peaks. The objective is for you (or your team) to have scored the most points at the end of the game when the expedition reaches the Summit.

At each Altitude Level, you make a bid for the number of tricks you think you will take and your goal is to reach exactly that bid without going over or under. If you reach your bid, you receive points; otherwise, you lose points.

—description from the designer

Scorpion Supreme is dead and the Subcommanders in the Scorp Corps are staking their claim to the title.

In Ruthless 86, you take on the role of a brash, outrageous 80s cartoon villain, like steampunk pirate Molly Roger, the enigmatic and unknowable Sylence, or Tyrannoczar, a super soldier cloned from the DNA of history’s greatest rulers plus a T-Rex. 
 Prove to your rivals that you deserve to inherit the Scorpion Supreme title from your mysteriously departed leader. Win the loyalty of a legion of disposable Scorpion Soldiers and send them on Missions around the World. Expand and fortify your HexQuarters, your remote lair. Commandeer your evil organization’s Doomsday Devices. Stab backs, 86 your enemies, and, most of all, get Ruthless.

Ruthless 86 is a dice builder worker placement game. Recruit and deploy Troopers to battle stations in your remote HexQuarters, or combine six on a die to form a unit and deploy them on Missions across the World. Successful Missions expand your reach and Fortify your HexQuarters. When you feel you've built up a strong enough army, deploy to The Ranks and attempt to be the first to rise to the top and become the new Scorpion Supreme.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Caverna: The Forgotten Folk – Adventurers Promo
description: Promo for the Caverna expansion The Forgotten Folk. Instead of dwarves as usual, a player can now also colonize the mountain as an Adventurer.

boardgame: The Good, the Bad, and the Munchkin: Complete Edition
description:
The Good, the Bad, and the Munchkin opens a whole new frontier for munchkins — the Wild West! Forget dragons and goblins — jackalopes and Davy Croc have just as much treasure to loot. Those plains may seem wide open, but there's lots of monsters to kill and lots of stuff to take.

Become a Cowboy with a Twenty-Gallon Hat, or an Indian on a Catamount. Fight Wild Bill Hiccup, the Killer Jalapeno, and a Barber Shop Quartet. Take The Annual Bath for an extra level. Or just pal around with your sidekick the Greenhorn, until you sacrifice him to distract a monster.

The Good, the Bad, and the Munchkin: Complete Edition contains the original base game, all the cards from the Beating a Dead Horse expansion, each Western-themed promo card released, and thematic bookmarks that each contain a new rule for play.

Hanabi — named for the Japanese word for "fireworks" — is a co-operative game in which players try to create the perfect fireworks show by placing the cards on the table in the right order. (In Japanese, hanabi is written as 花火; these are the ideograms flower and fire, respectively.)

The card deck consists of five different colors of cards, numbered 1–5 in each color. For each color, the players try to place a row in the correct order from 1–5. Sounds easy, right? Well, not quite, as in this game you hold your cards so that they're visible only to other players. To assist other players in playing a card, you must give them hints regarding the numbers or the colors of their cards. Players must act as a team to avoid errors and to finish the fireworks display before they run out of cards.

Hanabi Deluxe II is similar to the Hanabi Deluxe edition first released in 2013, which features the same gameplay as the original game, but with the cards being replaced by Mah Jhong-style tiles. Hanabi Deluxe II includes six unique tiles that comprise the "Master Artisan" expansion.

boardgame: Transformers Trading Card Game: Rise of the Combiners
description:
The Transformers Trading Card Game is a battling card game designed for two players. Players build a team of Transformers character cards and power them up with a customizable deck of battle cards. Transformers character cards are twice the size of standard playing cards; they can be flipped from bot mode to alt mode and back, and they feature premium printing treatment on one of their sides. The deck of battle cards features Action and Upgrade cards to enhance Transformers character cards.

Transformers TCG: Rise of the Combiners features the debut of combiners — character cards that combine to create a single giant robot! This expansion contains six combiner groups, including Predaking. Characters combine using new "Enigma" cards and by having all parts of the combiner in bot mode on the battlefield or in the KO area. Each section of the combiner may have an upgrade, and all damage from each section carries over to the new combiner bot.

This is a compilation of games, played with a spinner or roulette.

1. Bilder-Roulette (Picture-Roulette)
The 32 picture cards (animals) are are dealt up among the players. After the spin of the roulette, the indicated card is dicarded of. The first player to discard all his cards wins the game.

2. Buchstaben-Roulette (Letter-Roulette)
Before playing, the players chose a number of topics (city, country, ...). To beginn the game the spinner is moved to select a letter. Then time runs. Every player has to find words for the chosen topics with the indicated letter.

3. Zahlen-Roulette (Numbers-Roulette)
The goal of the came can be to get the highest or lowest numbers or to get a connecting line on a grid of numbers.

4. Karten-Roulette (Card-Roulette)
This game is played with a standard deck of 32 playing cards. The players buy cards for one coin a card from the game master. Then he spins the roulette 5 times. On the first spin the winning card gets gets the player two coins. On the next spins the players win 4, 6, 8 and 10 coins.

5. Immer mit der Ruhe
This is a standard Pachisi game, played with a spinner insead of a die. In addition the spanner may be used to determine which color is to be moved, so the players dont get their turns in regular intervals.

The journey begins and ends in Berlin. Movement is determined by a die. In some cities events hinder or help the players. The first player to return to Berlin wins the game.

This game was published as a promotional game for "Urbin" shoe polish between the first and second world war.

—user summary

This is the first expansion for the boardgame "Opération Archéo".
More scenarios; more items to collect; more archaeologists to join the staff. New mechanisms increase the challenge of your researches!

Crocodile Islands is a wooden dexterity game where 2-6 players take turns to place “crocodiles” onto islands but avoiding them “snapping” together.

The crocodiles have magnets embedded so they either attract or repel depending on their orientation.  The islands are player placed and as such can vary the amount of space available for crocodiles making the game variably more difficult (and likely shorter) or easier.

boardgame: Arkham Horror: The Card Game – In The Clutches of Chaos: Mythos Pack
description:
In the Clutches of Chaos is the fifth Mythos Pack in The Circle Undone cycle for Arkham Horror: The Card Game.

After your fateful investigation in Union and Disillusion, the state of the city continues to decay. Breaches in reality begin to rip through the fabric of the Earth, and a faint melody of discordant pipes can be heard throughout the streets of Arkham. Frightened citizens have sealed themselves in their homes, yet you continue to patrol the streets, desperate for some solution to end the chaos.

In In the Clutches of Chaos, doom does not accrue at the usual rate. Instead, breaches are made at various locations, marked by resource tokens. When enough breaches have been made a single location, an incursion occurs. An incursion adds doom to its location and causes the breaches to spread, opening rifts in reality at connecting locations. Your goal is to prevent incursions by removing the breaches that cause them, but this can only be done by activating the action abilities on each location. If you do not maintain control over the breaches, you may soon find yourself overwhelmed with Arkham falling into madness. You cannot allow that to happen!

—description from the publisher
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)
Game: Arkham Horror – The Card Game
Game: Arkham Horror TCG – The Circle Undone
Series: Arkham Horror Files (Fantasy Flight Games)

boardgame: Aeon's End: Echo Stone and Splinter Missile Promo
description:
Includes 21 card dividers and 14 player cards.

7 X Echo Stone is a gem that allows players to gain an additional aether on their turn if they have played another Echo Stone in the same turn.
5 X Splinter Missile is a spell that not only deals damage, but if an ally discards a card, allows players to divide the damage among any number of minions.

The promo were added to Kickstarter backers post campaign and were available to retailers as promotional items.

—user summary

The Tides of Time Demo Deck is great way to introduce players to this fast, fun, and easy-to-play game.
The demo deck contains just six cards, but that’s all you will need to understand and get a feel for how the game plays.

Butt Hurt is a card game played among friends that reveals the, sometimes uncomfortable, truth. This game is not designed for sensitive people who get butt hurt easily. Man up and have fun! Butt Hurt game is not intended to be taken seriously.The first Reader reads a question card and privately votes for the person they think best matches question. Each player privately chooses who they think the Reader will choose. Each player that matches the Reader receives a point. Whichever player received the most votes gets to keep that card to read at the end of the game. Each person will take turns being the Reader, playing two rounds of red cards (BAD) & two rounds of blue cards (GOOD). The player with the most points at the end of the game wins. At the end of the game, each person reads the cards they have collected as their story. For example: I am out of shape and likely to steal your stuff, but I am the best motivational speaker who will always answer your call.

—description from the publisher

Play through a day of life for someone suffering from depression. Work to complete a series of increasingly difficult tasks, all while fighting the lack of motivation that comes with depression.
The goal of Depression is to complete six goals of increasing difficulty.

On each turn, you will discard cards from your hand either to use special abilities, or to complete a Goal card. You will end your turn by refilling your hand to five cards.

Completed Goal cards offer you help in completing future Goals. If you draw a Depression card, you must place it on top of a completed Goal card. To clear a Depression card, you must discard three cards of different types, or five cards total from your hand.

You win the game if you complete all six Goal cards. You lose the game immediately if the Draw Deck runs out before you have completed six goals.

-description from designer

A tactical WWII wargame that was published in the first issue of JagdPanther magazine. Eight generic scenarios were included for portraying conflicts involving German, American, and Russian forces. MP-44 is currently available in PDF format as Amarillo Design Bureau has digitally rereleased all fifteen JagdPanther issues.

—user summary

Mp44 [sic] is a squad level (10-12 man) game of infantry combat in WWII. In many ways it is the illigitemate [sic] child of Panzerblitz and Grunt. But it is distinctive and in many ways superior to both ... In this game we have squads of 14 men, and we have teams of two ... Some may complain that the game is too close to Panzerblitz. Well, part of that us intentional. I saw no reason to require the gamer to learn a new game system when most are familiar with Panzerblitz. For that reason, I left the factors where they were on the counter, and used, for all practical purposes, the same CRT ... Besides, this game is sort of a companion to PzB, allowing you to get closer to the action. Basically, we had to contend with Bolt-action rifles, semi-automatic rifles, machine pistols, and machine guns. Assault rifles are also included, and the heavier fire factors are indicative of the fire-generation of these weapons. The Vietnam type infantry provided for the Americans include not only assault rifles, but Grenade Launchers - pocket cannon ... German paratroopers might carry rifles, smg, or assault rifles ... Any differences in the relative intelligence or training or minor differences in weapons are reflected in reduced or increased range, reflecting more or less disciplined fire.

—description from the publisher

description:
Kradia: The Garden of Adventurers is the first game fantasy themed RPG of Kradia series. It  is published in Chinese. In the first episode, there are 6 main acts and 2 side acts. Each act has its own story, map and boss, players can build the map with the wooden chess board.

Each player will act as an adventurer to explore the treasure continent “Kradia”, competing with other adventures for the greatest wealth. Through the journey, you may meet new companions to join your party, get useful items or treasure, battle monsters or other adventurers, and encounter the final boss. When the night comes, strange vision may happen and affect the whole map, the raid boss could move and attack the adventurers actively!

description:
Loot the caves of your opponents by avoiding to take the blow of club which will make you lose all your booty. To help you: Observe, listen and analyze what your opponents are doing ... unless they bluff you!

In each round, one player defends his cave (of 5 levels) from the attacks of the other player who want to loot him. Place your traps on the different tiles of each level and bluff (or not) the other players to try to hit them and keep your booty !!

The other players can try to loot you on different level of your cave with the risk to fall on a trap or stop the loot and secure their booty on a barrel.

After all the players have defended their cave, the player with the most important booty behind his screen win the game.

Butterfly Effect is a cooperative card game with the theme of butterfly effect.

Each player must cooperate with other players to tie themselves together with the items that were issued to the start and end given.

Please enjoy yourself with the very small opportunities that are normally thought to be negligible, but eventually become big things that can not be ignored.

Although Fast Factory , six cars will be shipped within the time limit, becoming a dwarf "Sashigoin" dripping somewhere.

If we do not ship 6 carriages within the time limit, everyone is game over.

In addition, "Sakigoin" are actually qualified and work at the factory. Let's ship more cars and parts than other dwarfs and win the Best Dodgers Award this month!

There are "tires", "bodies", "engines", "interior", "exterior", "options" on the card.

Among them, three types of cards, tires, body, and engine with color are called "key parts", and at minimum this card can be shipped as it will be a moving car if it is complete.

The upper three white cards can increase the added value of the car by installing it in the car being assembled.

There are two places to assemble cars, one for each "key parts", you can place any number of cards except key parts.

Players put out the cards in turn, and if all six key parts can be shipped and shipped, the game is clear.

The parts are the finest items (?) With which the dwarfs refer to various cars. Let's make fun cars with chaos that jumped over the genre!

—description from the publisher

Story and goal

All children are rooting in the leaves. Because under the colorful foliage  many animals have hidden - also fruits and cones are below it.

Each turn adds an extra sheet with a new theme mixed on the  bottom among the others.

Who finds it the first to root?

Preparation:

a) Sort out the leaves with pictures and put them in the bag.

The other leaves are distributed on the "forest floor" in the box bottom. Then you put the coins next to the Box ready.

The youngest player starts, then it goes clockwise continue.

If you are the youngest child, take the fabric bag and take out a sheet without  looking into it. Look at the motive on the underside secret, without the others can see.

Remember this motif and hide it under the leaves in the box  while the other players turn around.

If you have hidden the sheet, your teammates - with one hand - dig in the pile of foliage, just for this new one

To search motive. The leaves may be opened and closed

Be careful, but be careful when digging: Leaves must remain in the box.

Anyone who thinks they have found the new motive they are looking for calls the name of  the animal or the fruit.

     Was it correct, the player may take a gold coin. The sheet comes back in the box, in such a way that the picture is hidden.
     If it is wrong, the other players may (only) continue to dig,


until one  finds the right motive and gets a gold coin.

At the end of the game:

As soon as the last motive has been found in the heap of leaves and given the correct name, the game is over. Who has collected the most gold coins, wins the game! If two or more players have the same number of coins, they win together.

Have fun digging, grumbling, rustling and finding!

Introduction:

Ark of Atlantis is a worker placement style board game. During each turn, players work hard to collect resources, save creatures, and find artifacts all while trying to build a ship large enough to save all of their workers before Atlantis sinks. As players race against time and each other, Atlantis continues to sink around them. Players must juggle their time between building a ship to save themselves and risking everything in order to save all that Atlantis holds dear. Players receive bonus points for risking and saving precious items from around the island before they too are lost to the sea. 


The Goal of the Game:

The goal of the game is to acquire more victory points than any other player. Players receive victory points for building ship sections, collecting treasure tokens, and selling resources. Players lose victory points for each worker they were unable to save at the end of the game.

How Ark of Atlantis Plays:
 Players take turns placing their workers around the island. On a player’s turn they can collect resources, build ship sections, or save valuable creatures, treasures, potions, and rare books from around the island to gain extra victory points. Each time a player places a worker or a group of workers they must then move their time track marker forward for each worker they placed. In Ark of Atlantis, the turn order is constantly changing depending on where a player’s time track marker is positioned on the time track. If a player’s marker lands on or passes over an event space the player draws an event card and then resolves it by flipping over an island tile. The second time an island tile would be flipped it is removed from the game. The game ends once all island tiles have been removed. Next, players tally up their victory points by adding any bonus points or deducting points for each worker they were unable to save. The player with the most points wins the game.

-description from designer

Play the popular TV quiz now at home, and discover which team will become "the highest exponent". To make a chance to win the game, each duo has to bundle their forces and outdo themselves and not by a little.

Knowledge alone won't be enough in "Twee tot de zesde macht".
Associative thinking, reacting quickly and being well-matched make the difference between winning and losing. Play the well-known rounds from the TV quiz like the roster, the 4 answers, and double right.

—user summary

A hex-and-counter simulation of American football, focused on portraying only a portion of a full game. The "I go, you go" rules cover movement, collisions (resolved on a "CRT"), forward passes, and hand-offs. Each counter represents one football player defined by two factors: power and movement points. The simplified football rules do not cover fumbles or penalties.

Scrimmage IV was published in the first issue of JagdPanther magazine which has been rereleased in PDF format by Amarillo Design Bureau.

—user summary

...a whole [football] game would take months [to play], but for a quick series or a key play, here is a fun way to waste an hour.

—description from the designer

On January 17, 1781, Banastre Tareltons' [sic] Brigade of Light Infantry broke cover shortly after dawn, and faced a low hill sloping to a crest about 1/2 a mile away. A few hundred yards away were the American Rangers, the three light infantry companies that formed the pickets of the American position. Behind them, about half way up the slop stood 300 American Militiamen, none of them eager to face the enemy. But at the crest stood the American line. Six hundred regulars, they were the heart of the American force. Wearing bright blue uniforms, they stood in straight lines measured carefully by their officers. At the crest of the hill stood Morgan, the swashbuckling American light infantry commander. The British had chased him for two weeks, and now they had him, for he could go no further.

This game is at best an experiment in muzzle-loading warfare. If it works out, and I feel it has, we shall produce a line of such games.

—description from the publisher

A game depicting the American Revolutionary War battle of Cowpens. The game does not define its time or unit scales.

American units, except for cavalry, are deployed on the map. Cavalry units being off-map but are allowed to enter at any time. British units enter the map on designated turns during the first nine turns of the game. The turn order starts with one side firing, moving, and then designating bayonet assaults. This is followed by a defensive fire phase, after which the bayonet assaults are resolved. The opponent then executes the same phases which constitutes a completed game turn.

A CRT is used for combat resolution. Unit rankings include an attack factor, defense factor, movement factor, and range. Victory conditions are based on points received for destruction of units, ranging from 3 points for destruction of a militia unit to 10 points for destruction of a cannon unit. You win by scoring 10 or more victory points than your opponent. A margin of less than 10 victory points results in a draw.

The Battle of Cowpens was published in the first issue of JagdPanther magazine. While the rules and counters for the game were re-released in a 2012 PDF reprint of the magazine, the game's map could not be located and the publisher has asked for a scan of the map from anyone who owns the original edition.

—user summary

Do you have what it takes?  Can you achieve the rank of Grand Admiral before the blue-suited banal bureaucracy forces you to retire? Or will you heroically go down with your Heavy Cruiser in the midst of a hellfire of photon torpedoes and plasma cannon bursts?  All you need to do is advance 11 ranks, receiving 11 different ship assignments, while engaging in 22 different missions and acquiring up to 15 different skills.  You also are eligible for decorations and commendations of the highest order.  Sound like a lot to track? Nonsense! It is all contained on ONE page.  You roll. You write.  Hopefully, you get promoted . . . or at least live to fight another day . . .

—description from the designer

AWARDS & HONORS

2019 Charles S. Roberts Best Amateur/Print&Play Board Wargame Nominee
2019 Charles S. Roberts Best Amateur/Print&Play Board Wargame Winner

description:
Who's the Smart Ass among your friends and family? Find out with this outrageous, fast-paced party game. Where were you in the 1990s? If you lived it, you know it! Countries toppled, music exploded, and newsworthy personalities kept us guessing. Don't wait - yell out the answer as soon as you know it! '90s Nostalgia works great as a standalone card game, or as an expansion pack for the Smart Ass board game.

The start player draws a card and begins to read the eight clues aloud. All other players get one try at answering each card. When someone guesses the answer correctly, they collect that card but if no one answers it, the start player keeps the card for themselves. Play passes to the left of the start player. The first player to answer four Question Cards correctly wins.

Contents: 90 question cards and instructions.

A set of expansion rules, units, and scenarios published in 1973 in the first issue of JagdPanther magazine. The expansion is currently available in PDF form as Amarillo Design Bureau has digitally rereleased all fifteen JagdPanther issues.

Rules revisions address the rules on fire and movement, close assault, smoke rounds, and artillery attacks (without spotters).

New units depict forces of Finland, Belgium, Norway, Poland, Italy, France, Britain, Japan, and the United States. Additional German and Russian units are also added to the game, along with units for Korean War scenarios. The new units are supplemented by research notes and tables of organization and equipment.

Finally, the expansion includes 35 scenarios set during World War II and the Korean War, with majority of these depicting conflicts in the European Theater. Hypothetical scenarios include a post-war conflict between Patton and Montgomery (!) and a 1955 "border skirmish" between British and Russian forces.

—user summary

Seek raises a question against one person's Hide and will chase it...

Hide, Interwoven with bluff, run away. Seek out the other person and see through the truth.

Come and enjoy the strategy inference game to stimulate the brain cells.
